Quantitative Watchlist Analysis

Generated May 03, 2026 at 04:42 · Lookback: 2y · 18 tickers · Benchmark: SPY

For learning, not for trading. Cross-sectional metrics, factor scores, and backtests are descriptive of the lookback window. Past performance does not predict future returns. Single-period rankings are noisy estimates subject to multiple-testing concerns (Harvey, Liu & Zhu 2016).

📋 Cross-Sectional Summary

Watchlist contains 18 tickers; lookback window: 2y. Top performer: MU (+80% annualized); laggard: DIS (-3%); return spread of 83pp across the universe. Most volatile: MU (62% annualized vol); calmest: VHT (15%). Best risk-adjusted return: GOOG (Sharpe 1.27). Average pairwise correlation: 0.45 (low — meaningful diversification possible); highest pair correlation: 1.00. This is a descriptive snapshot of the lookback window; past performance does not predict future returns.

What this is: A condensed read of the dispersion across your watchlist over the lookback window — best/worst performer, most/least volatile name, and how clustered the universe is in correlation terms.

What it isn't: Stock picks. The summary describes statistical properties of recent returns, not predictions about future performance.

🔍 Cross-Sectional Screener

The headline section. Sortable cross-sectional metrics for every ticker in the universe over the lookback window. Click a column header to sort by it.

Annualized return / volatility / Sharpe are computed from daily log returns. Sortino only penalizes downside deviation. Calmar is annual return ÷ |max DD| — rewards consistency. Beta is regression slope vs SPY. Corr SPY is correlation with market. VaR/CVaR-95 are historical 5%-tail measures of single-day loss.

Reading caveat: These are descriptive, not predictive. A name with high recent Sharpe is not necessarily a good prospect — it might just be lucky, near a high, or have benefited from a regime that's about to end.

📊 Factor Scores (QVM+L)

What you're looking at: The Quality / Value / Momentum / Low-Vol (QVM+L) framework as practiced by MSCI, S&P, AQR, and modern multi-factor screeners. Each factor is computed for each ticker, then converted to a percentile rank within the watchlist universe. The diamond marker is the equal-weighted composite of the four factors.

How each factor is built:
Momentum — composite of 12-month-excluding-most-recent-month (Jegadeesh & Titman 1993), 6-month, and 3-month price returns. Higher = better.
Low Volatility — 12-month annualized return volatility. Lower = better (Frazzini & Pedersen 2014).
Quality — composite of ROE, profit margin, debt/equity, and max-drawdown stability (Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen 2019).
Value — composite of P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA. Lower multiples = better.

Important caveats: (1) ETFs lack standalone fundamentals, so their Quality and Value scores are unreliable — focus on Momentum and Low-Vol for them. (2) Factor returns are cyclical (MSCI 2024); momentum has crashed badly in 2009 and elsewhere. (3) Composite scores rank within this universe, not the broader market — a 90 here just means "best of these 18," not a strong absolute signal. (4) Single-sample-period rankings are noisy estimates.

All score columns are percentile ranks (0-100) within the watchlist universe. Higher = better. The four right-side columns are the underlying raw values. Click any header to sort.

Normalized Cumulative Performance

The chart: Each name's cumulative total return rebased to 100 at the start of the lookback window. End-of-line values let you read the relative performance ranking directly. A line at 130 means "up 30% over the window."

Watch for: the spread between best and worst performer (dispersion), whether the leaders changed mid-period (regime), and how the ETFs (smooth, low-amplitude lines) compare to single names (jagged, high-amplitude).

Risk vs. Return

The chart: Each ticker's annualized volatility (x) vs. annualized return (y) over the window. Dotted lines are iso-Sharpe contours — any name on the same iso-line earned the same Sharpe ratio.

Reading the chart: Names above the SR=1.0 line earned more than 1× their volatility in excess return — generally considered the threshold for "good" risk-adjusted performance over a multi-year window. Names below SR=0.5 earned mediocre risk-adjusted returns.

Important: A point above SR=2 over a 2-year window is rarely sustained out-of-sample. Mean reversion in factor performance is well documented (MSCI 2024).

Drawdown Comparison

The chart: Underwater curves for every name. A line at -30% means the price was 30% below its running peak on that date.

Worth noting: Drawdowns matter more than volatility in practice. A 50% drawdown requires a 100% recovery to break even. Single names can spend years underwater; broad ETFs typically recover faster. The deepest and longest-duration drawdown line should not be confused with the most volatile name — these are different things, and high vol with low drawdown happens when volatility is symmetric (frequent up-days as well as down-days).

Correlation Structure

The chart: Correlation matrix of daily log returns, with rows and columns reordered via hierarchical clustering so that highly-correlated names appear adjacent.

What to look for: Square-shaped clusters of red along the diagonal indicate groups that move together — likely a sector or factor exposure. Names that are correlated with everything (whole row red) provide little diversification. Names that have a row of mostly-near-zero values are the most diversifying additions.

Caveat: Correlations are unstable across regimes. The 2020 stress period saw correlations spike toward +1 across most equity sectors — exactly when diversification was most needed.

📐 Mean-Variance Optimization

The chart: The long-only mean-variance efficient frontier (Markowitz 1952) computed across your full watchlist. Each gray dot is one ticker; the green diamond is the global-minimum-variance combination; the blue triangle is the maximum-Sharpe (tangency) combination. Equal-weight and risk-parity reference portfolios are also shown.

The capital market line (dotted) connects the risk-free rate to the tangency portfolio. Combining cash with tangency gives you any point along this line. Anything below it is dominated.

Important warning: Mean-variance optimization is notoriously fragile. Small changes in expected return inputs produce large swings in optimal weights (Michaud 1989). Tangency weights are diagnostic, not actionable — they show you what the in-sample data implies, but out-of-sample performance is typically much worse than the chart suggests (DeMiguel, Garlappi & Uppal 2009).

Tangency weights are sample-period optimal. Out-of-sample performance is typically substantially worse (DeMiguel/Garlappi/Uppal 2009). Use as diagnostic, not as a target allocation.

Strategy Backtest

What you're looking at: A backtest comparing six rule-based strategies on the watchlist universe. Each curve shows what $10,000 grew to under each strategy with realistic 10 bp transaction costs.

Strategies: Buy & Hold equal-weights all names on day 0 and never rebalances. Equal Weight rebalances back to 1/N monthly. Risk Parity weights inversely to volatility (Maillard et al. 2010). SMA Crossover holds names where 50-day SMA > 200-day. Momentum 12-1 holds the top half by 12-1 trailing return (Jegadeesh & Titman 1993). Mean Reversion buys Bollinger-band-low names, exits at the high.

What to learn: If buy-and-hold beats the rule-based strategies (it usually does on a small US-equity universe like this one), that's empirical support for what Bogle, Fama-French, and SPIVA have argued for decades — costs and turnover destroy most rule-based edges in liquid US equities.

Strategy performance comparison

StrategyTotal RetAnn RetAnn Vol SharpeMax DDCalmar# Trades
buy_and_hold+102.6%+42.6%21.8%1.42-22.7%1.881
equal_weight+71.3%+31.1%20.8%1.09-23.2%1.341
risk_parity+51.7%+23.3%17.6%0.93-19.8%1.1822
sma_crossover+26.8%+12.7%17.4%0.43-23.5%0.5434
momentum+33.5%+15.7%13.2%0.76-14.0%1.1212
mean_reversion+75.3%+32.6%21.7%1.09-26.8%1.22137

⚠️ Methodology Caveats

Read this before drawing conclusions. The biggest biases that make most retail backtests misleading:

1. Survivorship bias (NOT fixed). yfinance only knows currently-listed tickers. Backtesting on today's universe misses companies that went bankrupt, merged out, or got delisted — all of which biases historical returns upward.

2. Tiny universe. Cross-sectional strategies (momentum, low-vol screening) work properly on universes of 100-500+ names. With 18 names you're at the edge of usefulness; results are noisy.

3. Single time period. The result you see is one realization of history. The same strategy run on a different period (1970-1985 vs. 2010-2025) often produces very different results. Watch the entire MSCI factor report (2024) — they document 10 years of underperformance for some factor styles vs. the cap-weighted index.

4. Look-ahead bias (FIXED). The backtester shifts strategy signals by 1 day before applying them, so you trade on yesterday's close, not tomorrow's information.

5. Transaction costs (partially modeled). 10 bps round-trip captures commissions and a typical bid-ask spread for liquid US equities. It doesn't model market impact, taxes, or short-borrow costs.

6. Multiple-testing. Try 50 strategies and one will look great by chance. Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016) argue t>3.0 is the right threshold for "real" signal under realistic multiple-testing assumptions.

Per-Ticker Technical Dashboards

Per-ticker drilldown. Click any name to expand its full technical chart: price with 50/200-day SMAs and Bollinger bands, RSI(14), MACD.

Honest warning: Technical indicators in liquid US equities have weak out-of-sample predictive power. RSI > 70 doesn't reliably mean "will sell off"; SMA crossovers don't reliably mean "will trend." Use these as descriptive context, not as buy/sell triggers (Bajgrowicz & Scaillet 2012).

📰 Watchlist News Feed

What it is: Recent ticker news from Yahoo Finance RSS, sorted newest-first across the entire watchlist (not grouped per-ticker). The ticker tag in front of each headline tells you which name it concerns.

How to read it: Markets typically price in publicly known information quickly (Fama 1970), so a headline you see in the morning has often already moved the price. Use this section for context — earnings, regulatory developments, executive changes — rather than as a basis for buy/sell decisions. For anything that matters financially, read the actual SEC filings via sec.gov/edgar.

Academic Literature

Curated foundational and contemporary papers organized around the methods this report uses.

Factor Investing & Cross-Sectional Returns

How equity returns can be decomposed into systematic 'factor' exposures. The intellectual foundation of the QVM+L scoring framework used in this report.
Fama, E. F., & French, K. R. (1993). Common Risk Factors in the Returns on Stocks and Bonds. JFE 33(1), 3–56.
The three-factor model: market, size (SMB), value (HML).
→ Original paper
Carhart, M. (1997). On Persistence in Mutual Fund Performance. JF 52(1), 57–82.
Adds momentum (UMD/Mom) as the fourth canonical factor.
→ Original paper
Fama, E. F., & French, K. R. (2015). A Five-Factor Asset Pricing Model. JFE 116(1), 1–22.
Adds profitability (RMW) and investment (CMA) factors.
→ Original paper
Asness, C., Frazzini, A., & Pedersen, L. (2019). Quality Minus Junk. Review of Accounting Studies 24(1), 34–112.
★ Definitive treatment of the quality factor and its construction.
→ Original paper
Frazzini, A., & Pedersen, L. (2014). Betting Against Beta. JFE 111(1), 1–25.
★ Theoretical and empirical foundation of the low-volatility anomaly.
→ Original paper
Harvey, C., Liu, Y., & Zhu, H. (2016). …and the Cross-Section of Expected Returns. RFS 29(1), 5–68.
Critical review: most published 'factors' fail proper multiple-testing thresholds.
→ Original paper

Momentum

The empirical finding that recent winners keep winning over 3–12 month horizons. One of the most-replicated cross-sectional anomalies in finance.
Jegadeesh, N., & Titman, S. (1993). Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers. JF 48(1), 65–91.
★ Original 12-1 momentum paper. The basis for the momentum window in this tool.
→ Original paper
Jegadeesh, N. (1990). Evidence of Predictable Behavior of Security Returns. JF 45(3), 881–898.
Short-term reversal: the 1-month return has the opposite sign — hence the 'skip last month' adjustment.
→ Original paper
Asness, C., Moskowitz, T., & Pedersen, L. (2013). Value and Momentum Everywhere. JF 68(3), 929–985.
Momentum and value as universal factors across markets and asset classes.
→ Original paper

Value

The persistent excess return earned by stocks with low price-to-fundamentals multiples.
Basu, S. (1977). Investment Performance of Common Stocks in Relation to Their P/E Ratios. JF 32(3), 663–682.
First formal documentation of the P/E effect.
→ Original paper
Fama, E., & French, K. (1992). The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns. JF 47(2), 427–465.
Documents value (book/market) as a robust factor; market beta alone is insufficient.
→ Original paper
Lakonishok, J., Shleifer, A., & Vishny, R. (1994). Contrarian Investment, Extrapolation, and Risk. JF 49(5), 1541–1578.
Behavioral interpretation: value works because investors over-extrapolate growth.
→ Original paper

Low Volatility / Min-Variance

The empirical anomaly that low-volatility stocks have earned comparable returns to high-volatility stocks while risking far less. Counter to CAPM predictions.
Haugen, R., & Heins, J. (1975). Risk and the Rate of Return on Financial Assets. JFQA 10(5), 775–784.
Earliest empirical challenge to the risk-return relationship implied by CAPM.
→ Original paper
Frazzini, A., & Pedersen, L. (2014). Betting Against Beta. JFE 111(1), 1–25.
★ Theoretical model: leverage constraints cause investors to overpay for high-beta stocks.
→ Original paper
Baker, M., Bradley, B., & Wurgler, J. (2011). Benchmarks as Limits to Arbitrage. FAJ 67(1), 40–54.
Why the low-vol anomaly persists despite being well-documented.
→ Original paper

Portfolio Construction

Theory underlying the efficient frontier and how to combine multiple assets into a coherent allocation.
Markowitz, H. (1952). Portfolio Selection. JF 7(1), 77–91.
★ Founding paper of modern portfolio theory.
→ Original paper
Sharpe, W. (1964). Capital Asset Prices. JF 19(3), 425–442.
CAPM and the capital market line.
→ Original paper
DeMiguel, V., Garlappi, L., & Uppal, R. (2009). Optimal Versus Naive Diversification. RFS 22(5), 1915–1953.
★ 1/N is hard to beat out-of-sample: estimation error overwhelms optimization gains.
→ Original paper
Michaud, R. (1989). The Markowitz Optimization Enigma. FAJ 45(1), 31–42.
Why mean-variance optimization is fragile in practice.
→ Original paper

Performance Evaluation & Statistical Inference

How to honestly evaluate whether a backtest's apparent edge is real or due to chance / overfitting.
Sharpe, W. (1966). Mutual Fund Performance. Journal of Business 39(1), 119–138.
Original Sharpe ratio formulation.
→ Original paper
Lo, A. (2002). The Statistics of Sharpe Ratios. FAJ 58(4), 36–52.
★ Standard errors and significance tests for Sharpe ratios with autocorrelation.
→ Original paper
Bailey, D., Borwein, J., López de Prado, M., & Zhu, Q. (2014). Pseudo-Mathematics and Financial Charlatanism. Notices of the AMS 61(5), 458–471.
Why most published backtests are overfit.
→ Original paper
Bajgrowicz, P., & Scaillet, O. (2012). Technical Trading Revisited. JFE 106(3), 473–491.
Out-of-sample test: technical rules don't survive transaction costs.
→ Original paper

Behavioral Finance

Why systematic factor anomalies persist despite being well-known: human psychology and institutional constraints prevent full arbitrage.
Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory. Econometrica 47(2), 263–291.
Foundation of behavioral finance. Loss aversion and reference dependence.
→ Original paper
Barber, B., & Odean, T. (2000). Trading Is Hazardous to Your Wealth. JF 55(2), 773–806.
Empirical: more trading activity → worse performance for retail investors.
→ Original paper
Shleifer, A., & Vishny, R. (1997). The Limits of Arbitrage. JF 52(1), 35–55.
Why mispricings can persist even when sophisticated traders see them.
→ Original paper
